threats.title: update_market Uses Current Block Number for All Historical Epoch Time Calculations, Enabling Retroactive Reward Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: In LendingLedger.update_market, the epochTime variable is computed using block.number (current block) inside the while loop rather than the iterator variable i (historical block being processed). This means gauge_relative_weight_write is called with the CURRENT estimated timestamp for ALL historical epoch iterations. An attacker can manipulate current gauge weights (via vote_for_gauge_weights) and then trigger update_market on a stale market to retroactively apply favorable gauge weights to all un-updated historical epochs, claiming disproportionate CANTO rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: The epochTime formula should use the loop iterator i instead of block.number. All historical epochs receive the same gauge weight as the current period, enabling retroactive weight manipulation for reward theft.

proof_of_signal.pattern: epochTime uses block.number instead of loop variable i; epochTime = referenceBlockTime + ((block.number - referenceBlockNumber) * averageBlockTime) / 1000

preconditions: Market has not had update_market called for multiple epochs (stale lastRewardBlock)
Market has positive lendingMarketTotalBalance
Attacker has veCANTO voting power to change gauge weights
cantoPerBlock is nonzero for the relevant epochs

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: epochTime computed with block.number instead of i in while loop iterating from lastRewardBlock to block.number
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: All iterations of the reward accumulation loop use the same current-block epochTime, causing gauge_relative_weight_write to return current weights for all historical periods
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-iteration time calculation; should use i instead of block.number

analysis_questions: How many epochs can a market remain stale before someone calls update_market?
What is the maximum reward inflation factor achievable by manipulating current weights?
Does gauge_relative_weight_write return the same value for all loop iterations within one tx?
validation_requirements: Calculate the difference in rewards between using historical vs current gauge weights
Verify that gauge_relative_weight_write returns current-period weight for future epochTime values
Test end-to-end attack: vote, wait for staleness, update_market, claim

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker votes to give a low-weight market 90% gauge weight. Calls update_market on a market stale for 10 epochs. All 10 epochs are credited at 90% weight instead of historical 10%. Attacker's proportional share yields 9x expected rewards.
prerequisites: Deposited position in the target market

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Massive over-distribution of CANTO rewards to attacker's chosen market. Could drain entire LendingLedger CANTO balance.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All legitimate users across all markets receive fewer rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult — rewards are sent immediately on claim; no clawback mechanism

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying epochTime accuracy per iteration in update_market

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas cost of update_market over many epochs
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient veCANTO to shift gauge weights significantly
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable on-chain via gauge weight changes before market updates

threats.title: GaugeController.remove_gauge Always Reverts for Gauges with Active Weight Due to Type-0 Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: In remove_gauge, gauge_types_[_gauge] is set to 0 BEFORE calling _remove_gauge_weight. Inside _remove_gauge_weight, gauge_type = gauge_types_[_gauge] - 1 evaluates to -1 (int128). _get_sum(-1) returns 0 because time_sum[-1] is uninitialized. The subsequent calculation new_sum = 0 - old_weight_bias causes a uint256 underflow revert in Solidity 0.8 whenever old_weight_bias > 0. This makes remove_gauge permanently non-functional for any gauge that has active votes.
threats.investigation_rationale: The ordering of state mutation (zeroing gauge_types_ before calling _remove_gauge_weight) is demonstrably incorrect. The function is broken for its primary use case.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gauge_types_[_gauge] = 0 executed before _remove_gauge_weight(_gauge) which reads gauge_types_[_gauge] - 1

preconditions: Target gauge has any positive weight (votes allocated to it)
Governance calls remove_gauge without first calling remove_gauge_weight

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State (gauge_types_) zeroed before internal function reads it; _remove_gauge_weight reads gauge_types_[_gauge]-1 = -1; _get_sum(-1) returns 0; new_sum = 0 - old_weight_bias underflows
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: remove_gauge is non-functional for active gauges — governance cannot remove malicious or deprecated gauges
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: gauge_types_ should be zeroed AFTER _remove_gauge_weight, or type should be passed as parameter

analysis_questions: Can governance work around this by calling remove_gauge_weight before remove_gauge?
Even with the workaround, does _remove_gauge_weight in remove_gauge corrupt storage by writing to type -1?
Are there any gauges in the system that cannot be removed at all?
validation_requirements: Attempt remove_gauge on a gauge with positive votes; confirm revert
Test workaround sequence: remove_gauge_weight then remove_gauge
Check storage writes to points_sum[-1] for corruption

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance deploys a gauge that turns malicious. Governance attempts to remove it via remove_gauge but the transaction always reverts due to underflow. The malicious gauge continues receiving emissions indefinitely.
prerequisites: Gauge has any positive vote weight

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Inability to remove compromised gauges means continued misdirection of CANTO emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All veCANTO holders and liquidity providers affected by malicious gauge emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partial workaround exists via separate remove_gauge_weight call, but governance may not know this

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for remove_gauge on a gauge with active votes

threats.title: Users' Vote Power Permanently Locked When Gauges Are Removed
threats.hypothesis_description: After a gauge is removed (gauge_types_ set to 0), users who had votes allocated to that gauge cannot reclaim their vote_user_power. vote_for_gauge_weights allows _user_weight=0 on removed gauges (first require passes), but then gauge_type = gauge_types_[_gauge_addr] - 1 = -1, and require(gauge_type >= 0) fails, causing a revert. The user's vote_user_power permanently includes the locked bps, reducing their effective voting capacity forever.
threats.investigation_rationale: The two require statements in vote_for_gauge_weights contradict each other: the first allows weight-0 votes on non-gauges, but the second blocks execution for non-gauges. Users can never recover allocated power from removed gauges.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_user_weight == 0 || gauge_types_[_gauge_addr] != 0) passes for weight=0 on removed gauge, but gauge_type = 0 - 1 = -1 fails require(gauge_type >= 0)

preconditions: User has previously voted for a gauge with nonzero weight
Governance removes that gauge (gauge_types_ set to 0)
User attempts to vote 0 on the removed gauge to reclaim power

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Contradictory require statements: first allows 0-weight votes on removed gauges, second blocks execution due to gauge_type = -1
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: vote_user_power[user] permanently includes locked bps; user voting capacity permanently degraded
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to reclaim vote power from removed gauges; no governance function to reset user votes

analysis_questions: How many users could be affected by a single gauge removal?
Is there any other path to reclaim vote_user_power?
Can governance deploy a new gauge at the same address to unblock vote reclamation?
validation_requirements: Vote for gauge, remove gauge, attempt to vote 0 — confirm revert at line 250
Check vote_user_power remains inflated after gauge removal
Verify no alternative recovery mechanism exists

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User allocates 5000 bps to gauge A. Governance removes gauge A. User's effective voting capacity is reduced to 5000 bps forever. After multiple gauge removals, users may lose all voting ability.
prerequisites: User has voted for a gauge that gets removed

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users lose proportional control over CANTO emissions direction; emissions become inefficiently allocated
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who voted for any removed gauge permanently lose voting capacity
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — no function to reset vote_user_power; no governance override

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for voting 0 on a removed gauge

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Users discover when trying to reallocate votes

threats.title: uint128 Overflow in accCantoPerShare Bricks Markets with Low Total Supply
threats.hypothesis_description: In update_market, accCantoPerShare and secRewardsPerShare are stored as uint128 and accumulated using +=. When lendingMarketTotalBalance is very small (e.g., 1 wei), the per-block accumulation value can be enormous. For secRewardsPerShare: blockDelta * 1e36 / 1 = 1e36 per block. After ~340 blocks, the uint128 addition overflows and reverts (Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic on +=), permanently bricking the market. All calls to update_market, sync_ledger, claim, and any LiquidityGauge transfer/withdraw revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The explicit uint128() cast truncates silently, but the += operator uses checked arithmetic that reverts on overflow. A first depositor with 1 wei can permanently DoS a market within minutes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: market.accCantoPerShare += uint128(...); market.secRewardsPerShare += uint128((blockDelta * 1e36) / marketSupply); with marketSupply potentially 1 wei

preconditions: Market is whitelisted with nonzero cantoPerBlock
Attacker is first depositor and deposits minimal amount (e.g., 1 wei)
~340 blocks pass without additional deposits (roughly 30 minutes at 5.7s/block)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: uint128 storage type for accumulators; no minimum deposit requirement; no overflow protection on accumulation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: secRewardsPerShare: 1e36/1 = 1e36 per block; uint128 max ~3.4e38; overflow after ~340 blocks. accCantoPerShare: similar with cantoPerBlock * relativeWeight / 1.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum deposit amount; no use of uint256 for accumulators; no overflow check before cast

analysis_questions: What is the exact block count to overflow for secRewardsPerShare with supply=1?
Can the uint128() explicit cast truncation alone (before +=) cause corruption even without += overflow?
Can other users deposit to increase marketSupply before overflow occurs?
validation_requirements: Deploy market with 1 wei deposit, advance 400 blocks, call update_market — confirm revert
Verify that subsequent sync_ledger and withdrawUnderlying also revert
Check if existing depositor's funds are permanently locked

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits 1 wei via LiquidityGauge into a newly whitelisted market. Waits ~340 blocks. Market is permanently bricked — all operations revert. If other users deposited before overflow, their funds are locked in the LiquidityGauge contract.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All funds deposited in the market are permanently locked; no withdrawals possible
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who deposited into the bricked market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — no admin function can fix the overflowed accumulators

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for small deposit overflow scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable only when update_market is next called

threats.title: De-whitelisting a Market with LiquidityGauge Permanently Locks All User Underlying Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: If governance sets lendingMarketWhitelist[_market] to false for a market with an active LiquidityGauge, all user funds become permanently locked. LiquidityGauge._afterTokenTransfer calls LendingLedger.sync_ledger on every token movement (transfer, mint, burn). sync_ledger calls update_market which requires the market to be whitelisted. When the whitelist is false, update_market reverts, blocking ALL token operations including withdrawUnderlying, transfer, burn, and burnFrom.
threats.investigation_rationale: The tight coupling between LiquidityGauge token movements and LendingLedger whitelist creates a denial-of-service when governance de-whitelists a market. Users have no escape path for their underlying tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(lendingMarketWhitelist[_market], 'Market not whitelisted') in update_market; called by sync_ledger; called by _afterTokenTransfer on every LiquidityGauge token movement

preconditions: Market was whitelisted with a LiquidityGauge deployed
Users have deposited underlying tokens into the LiquidityGauge
Governance de-whitelists the market

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: _afterTokenTransfer unconditionally calls sync_ledger; sync_ledger unconditionally calls update_market; update_market requires whitelist
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No fallback path for withdrawals when market is de-whitelisted; user funds permanently locked
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bypass mechanism for de-whitelisted markets; no emergency withdraw; _afterTokenTransfer has no try/catch

analysis_questions: Is there any way for users to recover underlying tokens without sync_ledger succeeding?
Could governance re-whitelist temporarily to allow withdrawals?
Are approve/allowance operations also blocked?
validation_requirements: Whitelist market, deposit underlying, de-whitelist, attempt withdrawUnderlying — confirm revert
Verify transfer, burn, burnFrom all also revert
Check if approve still works (no _afterTokenTransfer hook)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance de-whitelists a market to stop emissions (routine operation). All LiquidityGauge users are unable to withdraw underlying tokens. Funds remain locked until governance re-whitelists. If governance is compromised or keys lost, funds are permanently locked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total value locked in the LiquidityGauge is inaccessible
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors in the de-whitelisted market's LiquidityGauge
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Governance can re-whitelist to unlock, but if governance is unavailable, funds are permanent lost

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for de-whitelisting impact on LiquidityGauge withdrawals

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Users immediately notice inability to withdraw

threats.title: LiquidityGauge Exposes ERC20Burnable.burn() Which Permanently Destroys Underlying Token Backing
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidityGauge inherits ERC20Burnable, exposing public burn(uint256) and burnFrom(address, uint256) functions. Calling burn() destroys gauge tokens and triggers _afterTokenTransfer which calls sync_ledger(user, -amount), reducing the user's LendingLedger balance. However, the underlying tokens are NOT returned to the user — they remain permanently locked in the LiquidityGauge contract with no recovery mechanism. This violates the stated invariant that gauge tokens are backed 1:1 by underlying.
threats.investigation_rationale: The burn functions are unnecessary and destructive. They create an irreversible loss path that shouldn't be exposed. Any user or integrating contract calling standard ERC20 burn functions loses their underlying deposit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: contract LiquidityGauge is ERC20, ERC20Burnable — inherits public burn/burnFrom which destroy gauge tokens without returning underlying

preconditions: User holds LiquidityGauge tokens
User or approved spender calls burn(amount) or burnFrom(user, amount)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Public burn/burnFrom destroys gauge tokens; _afterTokenTransfer triggers sync_ledger to reduce ledger balance; underlying tokens not transferred back
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Underlying tokens are permanently orphaned in the gauge contract; no recovery mechanism
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: burn/burnFrom should be overridden to revert or should call withdrawUnderlying logic

analysis_questions: Can the orphaned underlying tokens be recovered by anyone?
Do integrating contracts or wallets commonly call burn on ERC20Burnable tokens?
What is the total exposure if users interact with burn?
validation_requirements: Call burn on LiquidityGauge tokens; verify underlying stays in contract
Verify no function exists to sweep orphaned underlying tokens
Check that gauge totalSupply < underlying balance after burn

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User calls burn(1000e18) instead of withdrawUnderlying(1000e18). Their gauge tokens are destroyed, their LendingLedger balance zeroed, but 1000 underlying tokens remain permanently stuck in the gauge contract.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of underlying tokens equal to burned gauge token amount
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user who calls burn or burnFrom on gauge tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — tokens are permanently locked in the gauge contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying burn behavior vs withdrawUnderlying

threats.title: accCantoPerShare Lacks 1e18 Precision Scaling Leading to Reward Truncation for Normal Market Conditions
threats.hypothesis_description: In update_market, the accCantoPerShare accumulation formula is: (blockDelta * cantoPerBlock[epoch] * gaugeRelativeWeight) / marketSupply. There is no intermediate 1e18 scaling factor before the division. Compare with secRewardsPerShare which scales by 1e36. When cantoPerBlock * gaugeRelativeWeight is small relative to marketSupply (e.g., low emission rate, small gauge weight, large market supply), the division truncates to zero, permanently losing rewards for those blocks. This is unlike standard MasterChef implementations that multiply by 1e12 before dividing.
threats.investigation_rationale: The formula relies on cantoPerBlock * gaugeRelativeWeight (up to ~1e36) being much larger than marketSupply. For markets with large supply and low gauge weight, precision loss accumulates significantly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (blockDelta * cantoPerBlock[epoch] * gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write(...)) / marketSupply — no intermediate precision scaling before division

preconditions: Market has high total supply (e.g., > 1e24)
cantoPerBlock * gaugeRelativeWeight < marketSupply for single-block delta
blockDelta = 1 (per-block granularity iterations)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Integer division without prior upscaling; numerator can be smaller than denominator
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Rewards silently lost to truncation; no excess tracking or correction mechanism
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing 1e18 multiplication before division (standard MasterChef pattern)

analysis_questions: What is the expected marketSupply range for whitelisted markets?
For realistic cantoPerBlock values, at what marketSupply does truncation to 0 occur?
Does secRewardsPerShare (with 1e36 scaling) prove the developers knew precision was needed but missed it for accCantoPerShare?
validation_requirements: Calculate reward loss for realistic parameters (1e18 cantoPerBlock, 1e15 relativeWeight, 1e24 supply)
Compare expected vs actual reward distributions over extended periods
Quantify cumulative truncation loss

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: With cantoPerBlock = 1e16, relativeWeight = 1e14, marketSupply = 1e22: numerator per block = 1e16 * 1e14 = 1e30 / 1e22 = 1e8. With claim dividing by 1e18: user with 1e22 tokens gets 1e22 * 1e8 / 1e18 = 1e12 per block = negligible. But if cantoPerBlock = 1e14 and marketSupply = 1e24: numerator = 1e14 * 1e18 / 1e24 = 1e8, fine. It becomes 0 when numerator < marketSupply.
prerequisites: Large market supply relative to emission rate and gauge weight

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Partial to complete loss of CANTO rewards for depositors in high-TVL, low-weight markets
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lost rewards cannot be recovered; CANTO stays in LendingLedger contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: No precision testing for edge-case market supply / emission ratios

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect; rewards appear lower than expected

threats.title: unlockOverride Enables Vote Power Multiplication via Cross-Address CANTO Recycling
threats.hypothesis_description: When governance enables unlockOverride, users can withdraw locked CANTO early. However, GaugeController is not notified and the user's gauge votes (vote_user_slopes, allocated power, scheduled slope changes) remain active until the original lock expiration. The user can transfer CANTO to a new address, create a new lock, and allocate fresh votes — effectively doubling their gauge weight from the same capital. This can be repeated across N addresses to get Nx voting influence.
threats.investigation_rationale: There is no cross-contract state synchronization between VotingEscrow withdrawals and GaugeController vote accounting. Gauge votes persist based on the original lock_end timestamp regardless of whether the underlying lock still exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: vote_for_gauge_weights reads VE slope/lock_end at vote time but never re-validates; VE withdrawal does not clear GC votes; scheduled slope changes in changes_weight persist

preconditions: Governance has enabled unlockOverride
User has active lock and has voted in GaugeController
User has access to multiple addresses

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: VotingEscrow.sol withdraw() clears lock but GaugeController vote_user_slopes remains; GaugeController.sol lines 284-285 store votes independently
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No callback or state sync between VotingEscrow.withdraw and GaugeController; votes persist after capital withdrawn
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Same CANTO capital generates multiple independent sets of gauge votes across addresses
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to invalidate gauge votes on VE withdrawal; no re-validation of VE lock in GC

analysis_questions: How many vote-recycle iterations are practical before the old votes decay significantly?
What is the aggregate weight amplification factor achievable?
Do the old votes properly decay based on the original lock_end?
validation_requirements: Lock, vote, withdraw (with unlockOverride), transfer to new address, lock again, vote again
Verify both sets of votes are active simultaneously via get_gauge_weight
Calculate total gauge weight from both voting sets

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker with 100 CANTO: lock on address A, vote 100% for gauge G, withdraw. Transfer to B, lock on B, vote 100% for G. Both A's and B's votes are active on G, giving effectively 2x weight from 100 CANTO. Repeat N times for Nx amplification.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Attacker can direct disproportionate CANTO emissions to their preferred gauge with a fraction of the required capital
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All other veCANTO voters receive diluted influence; rewards misdirected
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Old votes eventually decay, but attacker can continuously recycle

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for vote persistence after VE withdrawal with unlockOverride

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring same CANTO flow across addresses

threats.title: VotingEscrow Checkpoint Loop Iteration Limit (255) Is Less Than Maximum Lock Duration in Weeks (260)
threats.hypothesis_description: The _checkpoint function iterates at most 255 weeks to process slope changes and fill global point history. However, LOCKTIME is 1825 days ≈ 260.7 weeks. If no checkpoint is called for the full LOCKTIME duration, 5+ weeks of slope changes will be missed, resulting in incorrect totalSupply and voting power calculations. The same 255 limit exists in _supplyAt. While this requires ~4.9 years without any VE interaction, it represents a mathematical inconsistency that could manifest on low-activity chains.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop bound (255) should be >= LOCKTIME/WEEK (~261) to guarantee correctness. This is a demonstrable off-by-several error in the iteration limit vs the maximum lock duration.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < 255; i++) — 255 iterations * 1 WEEK = 255 weeks < LOCKTIME 1825 days ≈ 260.7 weeks

preconditions: No checkpoint, createLock, increaseAmount, or withdraw called for >255 weeks
Active locks exist with slope changes in the missed 5-week window

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Loop bound 255 < LOCKTIME/WEEK ≈ 261; slope changes beyond 255 weeks from last checkpoint are missed
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Global point history incomplete; totalSupply incorrect; voting power calculations affected
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Loop bound should be at least 261 (ceil(1825/7))

analysis_questions: On the Canto chain, is 255 weeks without any VE interaction realistic?
What is the quantitative impact of missed slope changes?
Does the comment 'Hopefully it won't happen' indicate known risk acceptance?
validation_requirements: Verify LOCKTIME / WEEK = 260 > 255
Simulate 260-week gap with active locks ending in weeks 256-260
Measure totalSupply error from missed slope changes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Contract goes 260 weeks without checkpoint. Slope changes for weeks 256-260 are not processed. totalSupply is overstated (slopes that should have been subtracted weren't). This could affect governance decisions based on voting power ratios.
prerequisites: 260+ weeks without any VE state-changing transaction
Active locks with ends in the missed window

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect voting power calculations could misdirect emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All veCANTO holders and gauge voters
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Multiple checkpoint calls could eventually catch up, but data accuracy is lost

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for extended checkpoint gaps approaching LOCKTIME duration

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Multiple checkpoint transactions may be needed for catch-up
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: totalSupply discrepancy detectable by off-chain monitoring

threats.title: LendingLedger.sync_ledger Lacks Access Control Allowing Any Whitelisted Market to Manipulate Arbitrary User Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: sync_ledger has no access control beyond the implicit whitelist check in update_market. Any contract address that is directly whitelisted (markets without gauges) can call sync_ledger with arbitrary _lender addresses and _delta values. If ANY whitelisted market contract is compromised, buggy, or malicious, it can inflate or deflate any user's balance and reward debt, stealing CANTO rewards from LendingLedger. The trust boundary relies entirely on ALL whitelisted markets being honest for ALL users.
threats.investigation_rationale: A single compromised whitelisted market can manipulate all users' reward accounting across that market, without any per-user authorization check in sync_ledger.
deployment_assumptions: All whitelisted market contracts are honest and uncompromised
threats.config_evidence: LendingLedger.sol whiteListLendingMarket grants unrestricted sync_ledger access to market addresses. No deployment script shows which markets will be whitelisted.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sync_ledger takes arbitrary _lender and _delta from msg.sender; only check is market whitelist; no per-user authorization

preconditions: A whitelisted market contract is compromised or malicious
The compromised market calls sync_ledger with inflated _delta for an attacker address

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No verification that _lender actually deposited in the calling market; msg.sender = market is sufficient to modify any user's balance
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Compromised market can inflate attacker's balance to claim all CANTO rewards
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-user deposit verification; no cross-check with actual market balances

analysis_questions: What types of contracts will be whitelisted as markets?
Do those contracts have upgrade mechanisms that could be exploited?
Is there a governance process to verify market contract security before whitelisting?
validation_requirements: Deploy a malicious market contract, get it whitelisted, call sync_ledger with inflated delta
Verify attacker can claim CANTO rewards for the fake balance
Quantify maximum extractable value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised whitelisted market calls sync_ledger(attacker, 1e30). Attacker's balance inflated to 1e30. Attacker calls claim and receives all accumulated CANTO rewards for that market.
prerequisites: A whitelisted market contract is compromised

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drain of LendingLedger CANTO balance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users across all markets lose pending rewards

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for malicious market calling sync_ledger with arbitrary parameters

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Control of a whitelisted market contract
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring sync_ledger events for abnormal deltas

threats.title: Missing delegate/undelegate Functions Render VotingEscrow Delegation Architecture Dead Code
threats.hypothesis_description: The VotingEscrow contract architecturally supports delegation through LockedBalance.delegatee and LockedBalance.delegated fields, LockAction.DELEGATE and UNDELEGATE enum values, and an explicit delegation code path in increaseAmount. However, no external delegate() or undelegate() functions are implemented. Users can never change their delegatee from self, meaning the entire delegation subsystem is dead code. The QUIT lock action also has no corresponding function, leaving users with no early exit mechanism other than the global unlockOverride.
threats.investigation_rationale: Multiple code paths handle delegation logic that can never execute. The missing functions represent a significant feature gap — users expected to delegate voting power have no mechanism to do so, and no individual early exit exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: LockAction enum includes DELEGATE, UNDELEGATE, QUIT with no corresponding external functions; LockedBalance has delegatee/delegated fields; increaseAmount has delegation branch at lines 271-281

preconditions: User wants to delegate voting power or exit lock early

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: VotingEscrow.sol lines 63-69 (enum) and lines 271-281 (delegation branch in increaseAmount)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Dead code: delegation branch in increaseAmount is unreachable because delegatee always equals msg.sender; LockAction.DELEGATE and UNDELEGATE are defined but unused
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Protocol cannot support vote delegation despite architectural design; users locked for 5 years with no individual exit; dead code increases attack surface and audit complexity

analysis_questions: Was delegation intentionally removed or accidentally omitted?
Does the dead delegation code path in increaseAmount have correctness issues?
Should the LockedBalance struct be simplified if delegation is not supported?
validation_requirements: Confirm no function changes locked[].delegatee from msg.sender
Verify increaseAmount delegation branch is unreachable
Assess if dead code creates latent bugs if delegation is later added

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users expecting to delegate their veCANTO voting power to governance representatives or DAOs cannot do so. Users locked for 5 years have no individual early exit mechanism. The entire delegation economics designed into the contract are non-functional.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users cannot delegate, reducing governance participation efficiency; 5-year lock with no exit creates liquidity risk
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade or new deployment

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test coverage for delegation or early exit scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately discoverable by users trying to delegate
